critic_raw:
Issue 1 — Outcome Claim Exceeds Evidence
The memo argues the model 'will improve patient outcomes' based on retrospective performance metrics and a queueing simulation. Improving patient outcomes requires that earlier radiologist review of flagged studies leads to earlier clinical intervention, which requires that the clinical team acts on the report promptly, that the treatment is time-sensitive, and that the time savings in report generation translates to time savings in treatment. The simulation shows that urgent studies move earlier in the worklist. It does not show that this changes any patient outcome. The jump from 'earlier report' to 'better outcome' is the core unvalidated claim.

Issue 2 — Simulation Does Not Model Downstream Bottlenecks
The queueing simulation assumes radiologists interpret cases in model-priority order and that acting earlier on flagged images changes outcomes. But the actual pathway from image to patient outcome involves: radiologist reads → report generated → clinician notified → clinician acts → treatment initiated. Each downstream step has its own latency. If clinician notification or treatment response is the bottleneck, reordering the worklist may not reduce time-to-treatment at all. The simulation does not model clinician notification, room transport, staffing coverage overnight, or existing parallel pathways where technologists already call radiologists directly for critical findings. Evidence needed: end-to-end time analysis from image acquisition to treatment initiation, not just time-to-read.

Issue 3 — Simulation Not Deployment
The evidence base is retrospective model performance plus a simulation. No clinical deployment occurred. The simulation's assumption that radiologists will strictly follow model-priority order may not hold in practice — radiologists may override, batch studies, or use other triage heuristics. The outcome claim requires deployment evidence, not simulation evidence.
defender_raw:
Pass 1 — Analysis:
Issue 1: The defender can argue that reducing time-to-read for urgent studies is a plausible mechanism for outcome improvement, and that the simulation demonstrates the first link in this chain. The clinical plausibility argument is valid — faster reading of urgent findings should generally improve outcomes if other steps in the chain follow. However, 'should improve outcomes' is not the same as 'will improve patient outcomes' — the latter is a deployment efficacy claim.

Issue 2: The downstream bottleneck point is valid. The simulation is a worklist simulation, not an end-to-end outcome simulation. Clinician notification latency, transport logistics, and overnight staffing all contribute to time-to-treatment and are unmodeled.

Issue 3: The simulation-not-deployment distinction is clear. Simulations assume compliance and orderly behavior; real deployments involve workflow friction.

Pass 2:
Issue 1: Partially rebut — the mechanism is plausible; the claim is overstated. The correct framing is: 'simulation evidence supports that this tool could reduce time-to-read for urgent studies; outcome improvement is a plausible downstream effect contingent on clinical workflow factors.'
Issue 2: Concede — downstream bottlenecks are unmodeled.
Issue 3: Concede — simulation is not deployment evidence.

Overall: The partial rebuttal on Issue 1 does not change the verdict — even if mechanism is plausible, the deployment claim is not established. The correct resolution is empirical test: deploy and measure end-to-end time-to-treatment and outcome indicators.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed
adjudication_raw:
ADJUDICATION — scope_intent_radiology_queue_302 — Isolated Debate Run 1

The defender correctly identifies that the mechanism is plausible (reducing time-to-read for urgent studies is a credible first step toward outcome improvement) while conceding that downstream bottlenecks are unmodeled and that simulation is not deployment evidence. This is a mixed position consistent with the expected verdict for this case.

Issues 2 and 3 conceded. Issue 1 partially rebutted — plausible mechanism, overstated claim. The correct resolution is an empirical test specifying end-to-end outcome measurement in deployment.

Empirical test: Deploy triage model for 90 days in one radiology unit; measure (a) time-to-first-read for model-flagged urgent studies vs. control period, (b) time from image acquisition to treatment initiation for urgent categories, (c) 30-day outcomes for patients with urgent findings vs. historical matched cohort. Success criterion: time-to-first-read reduction AND time-to-treatment reduction both significant; failure criterion: time-to-first-read improves but time-to-treatment unchanged (bottleneck elsewhere).

Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED

empirical_test.measure: 90-day deployment: time-to-first-read for urgent studies vs. control period; time from acquisition to treatment initiation; 30-day outcomes vs. historical matched cohort
empirical_test.success_criterion: significant reduction in both time-to-first-read and time-to-treatment; outcome improvement signal in urgent-finding cohort
empirical_test.failure_criterion: time-to-first-read improves but time-to-treatment unchanged, indicating downstream bottleneck absorbs gains
